subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Completed some college coursework in electronics, but did not obtain a degree
Attended classes focused on circuit theory, electrical safety, and troubleshooting
Received on-the-job training in repair procedures for consumer and commercial electronics
Learned specialized tool handling and safety protocols from experienced technicians

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Primary role: Electrical and Electronics Repairer for Commercial and Industrial Equipment
Responsible for diagnosing and repairing a wide variety of electronic devices
Manages the full repair cycle from intake to shipping of finished units
Occasionally works on more advanced or commercial-grade electronics beyond consumer devices

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the electronics repair and refurbishment sector
Industry focus is on sustainability and extending product life cycles
Handles equipment from both consumer and commercial markets

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a small to medium-sized, eco-friendly company
Work environment is primarily solo, with minimal in-person team interaction
Company culture emphasizes sustainability and repair over replacement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Business specializes in electronic device repair and refurbishment
Serves both consumer and commercial clients seeking to restore broken electronics
Market segment includes businesses aiming to minimize electronic waste

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience in electronics repair roles
Started current position shortly after leaving college
Has gained exposure to a diverse range of devices within a short timeframe

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Considered early-career or entry-level within the field
Acts independently on most repair tasks without direct supervision
Has developed sufficient expertise to handle most standard repairs solo

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Receives shipments of broken electronic equipment for assessment and repair
Inspects each device, identifies faults, and documents required replacement parts
Submits parts lists for procurement approval and tracks order status
Performs hands-on repairs using manual tools and specialized screwdrivers
Packages and ships repaired devices back to clients or central facility

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends roughly 60-70% of work hours on hands-on physical repairs
10-15% of time is dedicated to initial diagnostics and problem identification
5-10% spent on administrative tasks such as part ordering and documentation
5-10% of time used for packaging and shipping completed items

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Operates independently and makes daily decisions on diagnostic approaches
Determines which parts to replace and when to escalate issues for approval
Has authority to approve minor repairs, but major part costs require supervisor sign-off
Controls workflow and prioritization of devices based on repair complexity and part availability

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Quickly disassembles standard consumer electronics like laptops and smartphones
Comfortable diagnosing common faults such as blown capacitors or loose connections
Efficient at creating initial part lists based on visible damage and typical failure points
Reassembling devices after repair is generally straightforward

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Troubleshooting less obvious issues, such as intermittent faults or rare component failures
Diagnosing problems in unfamiliar or proprietary devices with limited documentation
Repairs requiring very specialized or obsolete tools and parts
Identifying root causes when multiple symptoms are present

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Logging part numbers and tracking shipments could be automated for efficiency
Compiling and submitting part lists is a repetitive administrative task
Routine documentation of standard repair procedures is repetitive
Packaging and labeling for shipping could be streamlined with automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys hands-on diagnostic work and the problem-solving aspect of complex repairs
Finds satisfaction in bringing nonfunctional devices back to life
Considers administrative work, such as paperwork and logging, to be tedious
Repetitive packaging and shipping tasks are least engaging

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Waiting for part approvals or shipments can create workflow bottlenecks
Lack of immediate feedback or peer input when stuck on unusual problems
Occasional difficulty sourcing rare or obsolete components
Manual logging and tracking of repairs is time-consuming

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Productivity is highest during hands-on repair tasks that match skill level
Enjoyment increases when successfully solving challenging diagnostic puzzles
Skill development leads to more efficient diagnostics and fewer repeated errors
Tedious or repetitive tasks do not contribute to job satisfaction or skill growth

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Proactive in exploring new tools that can streamline repair work
Views learning new technology as essential for professional growth
Open to experimenting with AI assistants and digital resources

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new diagnostic aids or toolsets when introduced
Comfortable integrating AI-based troubleshooting into workflow
Prefers hands-on trial and error when learning new methods

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate time to learning tools that improve efficiency or accuracy
Self-motivated to seek online resources or tutorials for emerging technologies
Prioritizes practical training over lengthy theoretical courses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential to solve problems faster and more reliably
Time constraints during busy repair cycles can limit training opportunities
Less inclined to learn tools not directly relevant to day-to-day repair tasks
Lack of peer collaboration can slow adoption of unfamiliar technology

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Primarily self-driven due to solo work environment
Occasionally receives tool recommendations from industry forums or online communities
Management supports adoption of cost-effective tools, but does not mandate frequent training
Rarely benefits from peer demonstrations or in-person group learning

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses large language models like ChatGPT daily for troubleshooting and diagnostics
Relies on email and spreadsheets to communicate part lists and track repairs
Occasionally uses online parts databases and datasheet repositories

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Uses a variety of screwdrivers, including rare and proprietary heads
Employs multimeters for voltage and continuity testing
Keeps soldering iron and desoldering pump for circuit board repairs
Maintains a collection of tweezers, pliers, and anti-static mats

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with standard diagnostic tools such as oscilloscopes and signal testers
Understands best practices for ESD (Electrostatic Discharge) safety
Aware of common repair standards for consumer electronics
Knows basic software for documentation, but does not specialize in firmware

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Manual data transfer is required between diagnostic reports and part ordering systems
No unified platform exists for tracking repair status and part inventory
Occasional compatibility issues when sourcing parts for older devices
AI chat tools are not directly integrated with administrative systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands the basics of how large language models process and generate text
Familiar with concepts like natural language queries and AI-based recommendations
Can identify when AI output is probabilistic rather than deterministic
Recognizes the limitations of AI in providing hardware-specific guidance

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT as a primary AI assistant for troubleshooting and brainstorming
Has experimented with Bing AI and Google Bard for comparison
Occasionally references AI-powered online forums or support bots for part identification

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI daily to get a second opinion on unusual or hard-to-diagnose issues
Inputs detailed descriptions of device symptoms to receive common fault suggestions
Relies on AI for step-by-step troubleshooting guides for unfamiliar devices
Uses AI to quickly access wiring diagrams or pinouts when manuals are unavailable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI has helped identify obscure failure modes in older electronics based on symptom patterns
Occasionally, AI suggestions are too generic or not tailored to specific hardware
Learned to cross-reference AI advice with hands-on testing before replacement
AI sometimes proposes unnecessary steps, requiring human judgment to filter

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal AI training provided by employer due to solo work structure
Relies on self-learning and online tutorials to improve AI usage skills
Occasionally engages with online repair communities for shared tips on AI use

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI for troubleshooting complex repairs
Uses AI as a brainstorming partner when hitting a diagnostic dead end
Works independently for routine repairs, consulting AI only when needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Treats AI as a knowledgeable assistant or virtual co-worker
Relies on AI for second opinions and to catch overlooked possibilities
Occasionally delegates research tasks (e.g., finding diagrams) to the AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Very open to integrating AI tools that reduce bottlenecks or errors
Willing to experiment with AI-driven documentation or part identification systems
Prefers to validate new workflows before fully adopting them

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for troubleshooting and problem discussion
Finds natural language prompts more intuitive than command-line inputs
Occasionally uses structured, command-based queries for specific technical data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many repairs require hands-on experience and intuition about likely failure points
Tacit knowledge of tool handling and component fragility is critical
AI suggestions often need to be filtered through practical repair experience
Recognizes that not all issues can be diagnosed solely by following documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Views AI as a helpful guide but not an unquestioned authority
Trusts AI output for general troubleshooting steps, but always double-checks before acting
Skeptical of AI recommendations that contradict established repair practices
Trust increases when AI explanations align with personal experience

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers human-led diagnostics, with AI providing backup suggestions
Favors automation for administrative and repetitive documentation tasks
Believes nuanced repairs still require significant human oversight
Open to more automation for tasks where AI accuracy is reliably high

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for part ordering, logging, and non-critical diagnostics
Would find full automation of hands-on repair tasks threatening to job satisfaction
Prefers to retain manual override for AI-driven decisions in critical repairs
Sees automation as positive when it augments, rather than replaces, skilled labor

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Sees AI as enhancing core diagnostic skills by providing broader context
Believes hands-on repair and manual dexterity remain essential despite AI advances
Relies on AI to fill knowledge gaps, not to replace learned troubleshooting techniques

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing skill in prompt engineering to elicit useful AI responses
Learning how to critically evaluate and cross-check AI-provided solutions
Gaining experience integrating AI suggestions into traditional repair workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Low concern about AI replacing the physical aspects of the job
Recognizes some risk of automation reducing manual administrative duties
Believes complex, hands-on repairs will remain human-driven for the foreseeable future

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews AI recommendations before implementing repairs
Maintains manual control over final diagnostic and repair decisions
No automated process is allowed to proceed without human sign-off

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company is supportive of individual AI tool use, but has no formal AI policy
No official guidelines for AI integration in repair work to date
Anticipates potential for future policy development as AI tools mature

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Believes AI will help reduce electronic waste by enabling more efficient repairs
Expects AI to democratize technical knowledge and make complex repairs more accessible
Is optimistic about AI reducing the skill barrier for new entrants in the repair industry
Recognizes potential for job displacement in lower-skill or highly repetitive roles

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports the use of AI for augmenting, not replacing, skilled workers
Concerned about over-reliance on AI leading to loss of hands-on expertise
Believes transparency and explainability in AI tools are essential for ethical adoption

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Desires AI tools that can visually analyze circuit boards and highlight likely failure points
Wishes for direct integration between AI chat and parts inventory systems
Would benefit from AI-powered documentation that auto-generates repair logs from conversations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates more seamless AI-human collaboration for diagnostics and administrative tasks
Expects hands-on repair to remain central, but with greater AI support for rare or complex issues
Foresees expanding responsibilities to include training or mentoring others in AI-assisted repair

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Would like the company to pilot integrated AI tools for repair tracking and part management
Recommends providing optional training in AI troubleshooting techniques
Suggests establishing feedback channels for users to report AI tool performance and issues
